teamObjective.explanation: With only two cards remaining, the immediate team objective is to get player 0 out of cards. Winning this lead and keeping the tempo maximizes the chance of playing the remaining card next.

partnerBelief.summary: No partner signal has been sent yet (the only public event is the new-game event). Partner holds 3 cards and is likely positioned to support the team finish, but I cannot assert any specific holdings.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each hold 3 cards and are likely to hold cards that can beat a weak lead. They will try to seize tempo and block the short-handed player, though I cannot claim specific high cards or trumps.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the Ace gives the highest chance of winning the trick and retaining the lead, which lets me play the weaker deuce on the next turn. In a 2-card endgame against opponents with larger hands, keeping initiative is more valuable than trying to save a high card.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the deuce now risks losing the lead immediately to a higher card from either opponent. If that happens, I would be left with only the Ace and no tempo, forcing reliance on partner to regain control in an endgame race.

risks: An opponent may still have a higher non-trump card or a trump that beats the Ace, costing tempo.
If the Ace loses, I am left with only the low deuce and may be blocked from finishing.
Partner may not be able to regain the lead if opponents coordinate to control the trick.
riskAssessment.mitigation: There is no risk-free play with two low cards, but leading the stronger Ace first preserves the most tempo and gives the best chance to play the deuce next while I still hold the lead.

notes: The decision treats all opponent/partner holdings as uncertain and is grounded solely in public counts and the new-game event.
